title: A Golden Year (2025): Gold's Price Surge - The Signal in the Noise!
gist: Damodaran argues gold cannot be 'valued' since it has no cash flows and is priced purely by demand/supply, mood and momentum; by every historical metric (Gold/CPI ratio, gold/silver ratio, comparison to stock returns) gold looks overpriced after its 57% surge to over $4000/oz in 2025, but a decade-long structural demand shift (ETFs, distrust of central banks, dollar weakness, Trump-era policy turmoil) may justify a permanently higher multiple.

insights.claim: Damodaran's four-way investment taxonomy (assets, commodities, currencies, collectibles) implies gold cannot be fundamentally 'valued' the way a stock or bond can, because it has no expected cash flows — only priced, based on demand and supply, mood and momentum.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Debates about gold being 'under' or 'overvalued' are pointless by this framework — investors should instead judge gold on pricing metrics (ratios to history) rather than intrinsic valuation models.
insights.quote: Since gold, absent cash flows, cannot be valued, arguing whether gold is under or over valued is a pointless one, just as it is for bitcoin.

insights.claim: Gold functions primarily as a collectible rather than a currency or commodity historically, because it is an inefficient medium of exchange (hard to verify authenticity, secure, and subdivide) and even its use in jewelry is often driven by collectible value rather than aesthetics.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Because collectible pricing is driven by scarcity, durability and desirability rather than fundamentals, gold's price swings should be expected to be sentiment-driven and hard to forecast with macro models alone.
insights.quote: It is as a collectible that gold has stood out, with governments, banks and individuals attaching value to it over time.

insights.claim: Warren Buffett's long-standing aversion to gold follows directly from his value-investing philosophy of buying undervalued assets, since gold cannot be assessed as under- or over-valued absent cash flows; Berkshire Hathaway's investment in Barrick Gold is not a counterexample because a gold miner has expected cash flows and is thus an asset, not gold itself.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Distinguishes 'owning gold' from 'owning gold miners' for investors who want gold-price exposure with cash-flow-based valuation discipline.
insights.quote: It is worth noting that Berkshire Hathaway did invest in Barrick Gold, but an investment in a gold mining company has expected cash flows and is thus an asset.

insights.claim: Gold's durability as history's longest-standing collectible rests on three properties: scarcity (finite but not fixed supply — about 244,000 metric tons existed worldwide at the start of 2025, with roughly 60,000 tons left in reserves, versus only about 10,000 tons for platinum, which is too scarce to be widely held), chemical durability (does not corrode or oxidize), and psychological desirability (mythologized across cultures, from Midas to El Dorado).
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: This scarcity/durability/desirability framework can be applied to assess other collectibles (art, Pokemon cards, other precious metals) as alternative stores of value.
insights.quote: At the start of 2025, there were approximately 244,000 metric tons of gold in the world

insights.claim: Gold prices rose 57% through October 24, 2025, crossing $4000/oz in October and reaching $4118/oz by month end, with gains in every quarter of the year, building on a recovery that began in 2022.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A broad-based, multi-quarter rally (not a single spike) signals this is a sustained repricing rather than a short-lived panic trade.
insights.quote: Through October 24, 2025, gold prices are up 57% for the year, posting significant increases every quarter of the year.

insights.claim: Even after adjusting for inflation, gold's October 24, 2025 price hit an all-time real high (using year-end price comparisons), surpassing the inflation-adjusted peaks of the 1970s gold boom.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: This is not just a nominal record driven by currency debasement — gold buyers are getting genuinely more purchasing power per ounce than at any prior point in the data series back to 1963.

insights.claim: Gold's historical volatility is extreme even by its own standards: prices rose 133% in 1979 and then fell 32.15% in 1981, showing the best and worst years occurred within two years of each other.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors chasing the current rally should recognize that gold booms have historically reversed sharply and quickly, not just faded slowly.
insights.quote: gold prices were up 133% in 1979 and down 32.15% in 1981

insights.claim: Before 1971, gold prices were essentially fixed and stagnant because the US dollar was convertible into gold at $35/oz under Bretton Woods; once that convertibility was abandoned in 1971, gold prices became purely a function of market demand and supply.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: All meaningful gold price history for demand/supply-driven analysis effectively starts in 1971 — pre-1971 data is not informative about free-market gold pricing dynamics.
insights.quote: In 1971, the United States abandoned that backing, and gold prices have been set by demand and supply since.

insights.claim: Regressing annual gold returns against inflation from 1963 onward yields %Change in gold = -0.06 + 3.92(inflation rate) with only an 18.8% R-squared, and most of that explanatory power disappears if the 1970s are excluded — meaning gold is a much weaker inflation hedge than commonly believed outside of hyperinflationary episodes.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors buying gold as a general inflation hedge should recalibrate expectations — it only reliably protects against extreme, unexpected inflation spikes, not moderate inflation.
insights.quote: gold is more a hedge against extreme (and unexpected) movements in inflation and does not really provide much protection against smaller inflation changes

insights.claim: Gold prices correlate weakly with equity risk premiums (%Change in gold = -0.13 + 5.21(ERP), R²=5.02%) and show almost no relationship with bond default spreads (R²=0.20%), indicating gold tracks equity-market fear more than credit-market stress, but only modestly.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Gold is a noisy, unreliable short-term crisis hedge in normal-risk environments; it only becomes a meaningfully effective hedge during rare, catastrophic events.
insights.quote: gold prices seem to move more with fear in the equity markets than with concerns in the bond market, with every 1% increase in the equity risk premium translating into an increase of 5.21% in gold prices

insights.claim: Real interest rates are the strongest of the three fundamental drivers tested: %Change in gold = 0.18 - 4.69(real T-bond rate), R²=21.9%, meaning high real rates raise the opportunity cost of holding non-yielding gold and depress its price, while low or negative real rates push gold higher.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Watch real Treasury yields (nominal yield minus inflation) as the single best macro signal for gold direction — falling real rates are the most statistically supported bullish catalyst for gold in this dataset.
insights.quote: High real interest rates are negative for gold prices and low real interest rates, or negative real interest rates, push gold prices higher.

insights.claim: Gold has had only three standout decades of returns — 1971-1980, 2001-2010, and 2021-2025 — with unexpectedly high inflation driving the first and third, while the 2001-2010 surge is only partly explained by the 2008 crisis since gold rallied in the years leading up to it too.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The current 2021-2025 gold rally fits the historical pattern of inflation-driven boom decades, but its persistence into 2025 without a matching inflation spike is unusual and worth investigating further.

insights.claim: The Gold/CPI ratio hit 17.81 on October 24, 2025 ($4118/oz gold divided by a CPI index of 324.80), far above the historical median of 2.93 for 1963-2024 and 3.77 for 1971-2024, indicating gold is expensive by this simple valuation-relative-to-inflation metric.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A Gold/CPI ratio roughly 4-6x its historical median is a strong quantitative overvaluation signal for anyone considering adding gold at current prices.

insights.claim: Even after correcting for today's equity risk premium (which explains 47.7% of the variance in the Gold/CPI ratio historically), the regression-implied Gold/CPI ratio is only 3.19 versus the actual 17.81 — meaning gold looks significantly overpriced even adjusting for elevated market fear.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: This is the article's clearest overvaluation call — the gap between predicted (3.19) and actual (17.81) Gold/CPI ratio is too large to attribute to elevated equity risk premiums alone, suggesting a structural repricing rather than a fear-driven spike.
insights.quote: Put simply, gold looks overpriced in October 2025, even after correcting for changing equity risk premiums.

insights.claim: The gold-to-silver price ratio stood at 84.73 at the end of October 2025 ($4118/oz gold vs $47.80/oz silver), well above the 1963-2024 historical median of 57.09, suggesting gold is also overpriced relative to silver, though the two metals tend to move together, limiting the reliability of this signal.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A stretched gold/silver ratio could support a relative-value trade (long silver/short gold) for traders, though co-movement risk means it is not a clean signal on its own.

insights.claim: Gold has been overpriced by historical mean-reversion metrics for roughly a decade, with its price nearly quadrupling from $1060/oz at the end of 2015 to $4118/oz on October 24, 2025 — a persistence that raises the question of whether the pricing metrics themselves are outdated rather than gold being in a bubble.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: When an asset stays 'overpriced' by a decade-old model for ten straight years, investors should question the model's assumptions (structural demand shift) rather than assume an imminent correction is coming.
insights.quote: When an investment stays overpriced for that long, it is legitimate to question whether the pricing metric is flawed, and whether there a structural shift has occurred that has shifted the distribution.

themes.theme: Structural demand shift behind the decade-long rally

insights.claim: The rise of Gold ETFs has structurally expanded gold demand by removing the historical barriers of physical storage costs and the inability to make fractional investments, giving a much broader investor base access to gold exposure than in prior decades.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Gold ETF flows are a structural, likely-permanent source of incremental demand that should be factored into any mean-reversion valuation model for gold going forward.

insights.claim: Post-2008 central bank activism has eroded trust in central banks and governments for a segment of investors, pushing them toward both gold and bitcoin as alternative stores of value outside the traditional financial system.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Gold and bitcoin should be watched as substitute or complementary 'distrust' trades — flows into one may partly explain otherwise-unexplained moves in the other.

insights.claim: A decade or more of stress on the US economy and dollar — including the US credit ratings downgrade — has weakened the dollar's role as the default safe-haven currency, and with no other global currency ready to take its place, gold is reasserting itself as the alternative safe haven, which helps explain sustained price gains.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A weakening dollar-reserve-currency narrative is a structural, multi-year tailwind for gold that investors should track via US credit ratings, deficit trends, and dollar index moves rather than short-term news.
insights.quote: It is undeniable that the US economy and dollar have been under stress for the last decade or more, with the ratings downgrade for the US being only a manifestation of these stresses.

insights.claim: 2025's tariff threats and economic conflict under the Trump administration have raised the perceived odds of a catastrophic disruption to the post-war economic order, and this elevated tail-risk perception is an additional, more recent tailwind for gold prices layered on top of the longer-running structural shifts.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Trade-policy escalation headlines in 2025-2026 should be watched as a live catalyst for further gold demand from investors hedging tail risk, independent of inflation or rate moves.
insights.quote: as tariff threats and economic wars threaten to unravel an economic world order that has governed markets and economies for much of the last century

themes.theme: How gold fits (or doesn't) in a portfolio

insights.claim: Gold returned a 5.35% annual compounded return between 1984 and 2024, versus 11.38% for US stocks, meaning a 1984 investment in US stocks would be worth roughly ten times as much by 2024 as an equivalent investment in gold — while gold also had higher standard deviation in annual returns, making it a poor core holding on a pure risk-adjusted return basis.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors considering gold as a core (not insurance) holding should recognize they are trading away roughly 6 percentage points of annual compounded return and accepting higher volatility, a cost that is only rational if they define risk as catastrophic loss rather than standard deviation.

insights.claim: For investors using gold purely as portfolio insurance against inflation or crisis risk, a small allocation is insufficient — based on the historical return relationship between stocks and gold, a meaningful hedge requires a 15-20% gold allocation, not the commonly recommended 5%.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Advisors and investors sizing a gold 'insurance' position at the typical 5% allocation are likely under-hedged relative to what the historical data implies is needed to move the needle in a crisis.
insights.quote: given the history of stock and gold returns, a gold holding that is 5% of your portfolio will not be enough and you will need a holding closer to 15-20%.

insights.claim: Gold price moves have historically been contemporaneous with inflation and crisis measures rather than leading indicators, so investors hoping to use rising gold prices as an early warning signal to reposition portfolios (e.g., shifting from long bonds to short-term bills, or toward companies with pricing power) will generally not get advance notice.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Don't rely on gold price spikes as a leading indicator to reposition a portfolio ahead of inflation or a crisis — by the time gold moves, the risk has typically already materialized.
insights.quote: Much of the relationship, though, is contemporaneous, i.e,, gold prices rise in periods when inflation is high and risks surge, and there is only weak evidence of gold prices being a leading indicator of future changes.

insights.claim: Traders who bought gold in 2022 have profited from a three-year price surge into 2025, but history warns that gold-trading windfalls can reverse quickly and completely — many investors who got rich riding the 1977-1979 gold boom lost it all within the following five years.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors currently riding gold's 2022-2025 rally as a trade should have a defined exit discipline, since gold booms have historically given back gains as fast as they built them.
insights.quote: many investors who became rich riding the gold price boom from 1977-1979 lost it all in next five years

themes.theme: Who's in the gold trade now

insights.claim: Gold has historically been a niche investment held by a small subset of investors (skeptics of fiat currency and financial markets), but that niche expands during periods of instability, and Damodaran suggests the 2025 rally has drawn in mainstream, prominent market figures who publicly worry markets are in a bubble.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: If high-profile figures like Dimon and Dalio who warn of a market bubble are also allocating to gold, that convergence of bubble-warning rhetoric and gold buying is itself a sentiment indicator worth monitoring for a broader risk-off rotation.
insights.quote: if Jamie Dimon and Ray Dalio actually mean what they say about markets being a bubble, would it not make sense for them to hold gold?
